title: Small Modular Reactor Construction All Risks Market Research Report 2033
text:
Segments - by Coverage Type (Physical Damage, Third-Party Liability, Delay in Start-Up, and Others), Reactor Type (Light Water Reactors, Heavy Water Reactors, Fast Neutron Reactors, and Others), Application (Energy & Power, Industrial, Research, and Others), End-User (Utilities, Government, Private Companies, and Others), and Region (Asia Pacific, North America, Latin America, Europe, and Middle East & Africa) - Global Industry Analysis, Growth, Share, Size, Trends, and Forecast 2025-2033
+ Read MoreThe global Small Modular Reactor (SMR) Construction All Risks insurance market was valued at $3.8 billion in 2025 and is projected to expand to $7.2 billion by 2033, representing a compound annual growth rate of 8.6%. This market encompasses comprehensive insurance coverage for SMR construction projects across all risk categories including Physical Damage, Third-Party Liability, Delay in Start-Up, and specialized coverage types. The rapid expansion of SMR construction activities globally, driven by increased demand for decentralized power generation and carbon-free energy solutions, has intensified the need for robust all-risks insurance frameworks. NuScale Power's NuScale Micro-Reactors, Westinghouse Electric Company's eVinci reactors, GE Hitachi Nuclear Energy's PRISM technology, and Rolls-Royce's Rolls-Royce Small Modular Reactors represent leading technological platforms that require comprehensive insurance coverage throughout their construction phases. Regulatory bodies including the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC), the European Commission's Joint Research Centre, and China's National Energy Administration have established stringent safety and construction standards, making specialized insurance products indispensable. The convergence of SMR commercialization timelines, large capital investments exceeding $1 billion per project, and complex multi-year construction schedules creates substantial insurable risks that drive demand for innovative all-risks insurance solutions across Industrial, Energy & Power, and Research applications.
The primary growth catalyst for the SMR Construction All Risks insurance market centers on the accelerated commercialization of modular reactor technologies and their deployment across diverse geographic and sectoral applications. The global transition toward distributed energy generation systems has created unprecedented demand for SMRs, particularly in regions with limited grid infrastructure capacity and remote industrial facilities. Countries including the United States, Canada, the United Kingdom, France, South Korea, Japan, and China have announced substantial investments in SMR development and construction, with combined project pipelines exceeding 150 operational and planned units over the forecast period. The modular design of SMRs necessitates innovative construction risk management strategies distinct from large conventional nuclear facilities, creating specialized insurance market opportunities. Construction timelines for SMR units range from 36 to 60 months depending on reactor type and site complexity, during which multiple catastrophic risks including weather damage, worker injuries, supply chain disruptions, environmental liabilities, and project delays accumulate. The typical all-risks insurance premium for a single SMR construction project ranges from $15 million to $45 million depending on project scale, site characteristics, and regulatory jurisdiction. Insurance providers must develop specialized underwriting capabilities to assess geological risks, seismic vulnerability, hydrological exposure, and regulatory compliance requirements unique to SMR construction. The distributed energy generation sector has emerged as a primary driver for SMR adoption, particularly in industrial heat applications and remote power generation scenarios, amplifying the demand for comprehensive construction insurance products that address both conventional construction hazards and nuclear-specific liabilities.
This analysis is based on primary and secondary research conducted through Q4 2025, with data verified as of May 2026.
Asia Pacific dominated the global SMR Construction All Risks insurance market with $1.61 billion in market value during 2025, representing 42.3% of global revenue. China's commitment to deploying CNNC's ACPR50S reactors, South Korea's pursuit of the SMART reactor program through Korea Hydro & Nuclear Power (KHNP), Japan's exploration of Mitsubishi Heavy Industries' HTGRs for process heat applications, and India's expanding small reactor initiatives under the Department of Atomic Energy have created substantial insurance demand. Asia Pacific projects under construction or planning phases encompass over 48 SMR units with combined capacity exceeding 23 GW, driving sustained demand for construction period risk coverage spanning Physical Damage protection against typhoons and monsoon impacts, Third-Party Liability coverage addressing population density concerns, and Delay in Start-Up insurance mitigating the impact of regulatory reviews and grid connection delays. Regional insurance underwriters including Japanese carriers with nuclear sector expertise and regional mutual insurance associations have expanded capacity to accommodate Asia Pacific SMR growth, while international specialty insurers from Lloyd's of London establish regional partnerships to distribute construction risk across global markets. The concentration of manufacturing capabilities for SMR components in Asia Pacific, particularly in China, South Korea, and Japan, necessitates specialized supply chain interruption and equipment breakdown coverage extending beyond conventional construction insurance frameworks.
North America represents the second-largest regional market at 28.9% market share, driven by NuScale Power's Utah Associated Municipal Power Systems (UAMPS) project targeting first-of-a-kind commercial deployment of 12 x 77 MW SMR units. The United States regulatory environment, governed by the Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) under 10 CFR Part 52 licensing frameworks, establishes predictable approval timelines supporting investor confidence and insurance market development. TerraPower's fast neutron reactor initiative in Wyoming, Holtec International's multi-module designs targeting energy and industrial process heat markets, and numerous utility partnerships spanning midwestern and southern U.S. states have expanded the addressable insurance market. Canadian regulatory oversight through the Canadian Nuclear Safety Commission (CNSC) and Candu Energy's CANDU-based SMR designs for decommissioning site repurposing create distinct regional insurance requirements. North American construction projects benefit from established insurance markets with deep nuclear expertise, competitive premium environments, and sophisticated risk modeling capabilities, though premiums remain elevated due to seismic considerations on coastal sites and hurricane exposure affecting eastern and gulf region installations. Private sector participation through companies including Duke Energy, Dominion Energy, and Exelon Corporation seeking SMR deployments for grid stability and industrial process heat applications has accelerated North American market development.
Europe commanded 16.8% market share through strong regulatory frameworks under the European Union's Green Taxonomy and the European Commission's support for SMR commercialization. The United Kingdom's Advanced Boiling Water Reactor (ABWR) deployments and Rolls-Royce's distributed power station concept targeting existing coal and fossil fuel generation sites represent major construction insurance drivers. France's commitment to maintaining nuclear's role under the EU Green Deal, combined with Framatome's SMR development initiatives and EDF's exploration of modular reactor integration, has sustained regional insurance demand. The Euratom framework, established under EU regulations, provides harmonized safety standards across member states but creates insurance complexity requiring policy structures addressing multi-jurisdictional regulatory requirements. European insurance markets traditionally possess conservative underwriting practices for nuclear construction risks, resulting in more restrictive policy terms and higher deductibles compared to North American markets. Eastern European expansion opportunities in Czech Republic, Poland, and Romania, pursuing SMR deployment for industrial process heat decarbonization, are beginning to attract insurance market attention and specialty capacity. Latin America maintained 7.2% market share through Argentina and Brazil's renewable energy diversification strategies incorporating SMR components, while Middle East & Africa represented 4.8% market share driven by nascent nuclear programs in the United Arab Emirates and South Africa exploring SMR applications for remote mining operations and desalination processes.
The global shift toward decentralized power generation has catalyzed unprecedented investment in Small Modular Reactor technology development and commercial deployment. Approximately 140 SMR units are currently in planning, construction, or pre-operational phases across 15+ countries, with combined generating capacity targeting 73 GW by 2035. Major projects including NuScale Power's Carbon Free Power Project in Idaho, Westinghouse's eVinci deployment in Poland, X-energy's Xe-100 plants in Canada, and Kairos Power's molten salt reactors in Tennessee represent construction activities requiring comprehensive all-risks insurance coverage spanning 48 to 72-month project timelines. The modular construction approach reduces on-site assembly time compared to conventional reactors, but introduces fragmented risk profiles requiring specialized underwriting models to address factory manufacturing risks, transportation and logistics hazards, sequential module installation complexities, and interconnection scheduling risks. Insurance demand scales directly with project count and construction timeline duration, positioning underwriters to capture 8 to 12 percentage point annual premium growth as projects transition from planning to active construction phases.
Regulatory bodies worldwide have established increasingly stringent safety standards and financial responsibility requirements for nuclear construction projects, mandating comprehensive insurance coverage as a precondition for construction licensing and operating permits. The U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission requires construction period liability insurance of at least $350 million covering Public Liability exposures, the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) safety standards impose detailed Construction Safety Programme requirements, and the Convention on Nuclear Safety established under international treaty requires financial security mechanisms including insurance. The European Union's Environmental Liability Directive 2004/35/EC imposes strict liability for environmental damage from nuclear activities, necessitating Environmental Liability and Remediation Cost Overrun insurance. The OECD Nuclear Energy Agency has published comprehensive guidance on Construction Risk Management for Advanced Reactors emphasizing insurance as a critical financial protection mechanism. Regulatory approval timelines extend 24 to 36 months prior to construction commencement, during which insurance capacity building occurs. The integration of insurance requirements into the licensing framework transforms insurance from an optional risk management tool into a mandatory regulatory compliance mechanism, supporting sustained demand growth independent of broader market cycles.
SMRs have emerged as viable solutions for industrial process heat applications including hydrogen production, synthetic fuel manufacturing, district heating, and steel production decarbonization, sectors historically dependent on fossil fuel heat sources. The International Energy Agency projects industrial heat demand will comprise approximately 38% of total energy requirements by 2030, with low-carbon heat solutions including SMRs addressing regulatory pressures under the EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism and equivalent decarbonization frameworks in North America and Asia. Companies including Ørsted, ArcelorMittal, and Saudi Aramco are evaluating SMR deployment for industrial process heat, creating new end-user categories and expanding the addressable insurance market beyond traditional Utilities and Government users. The industrial application focus drives demand for Construction Period Business Interruption insurance specific to heat delivery infrastructure, Machinery Breakdown coverage for specialized industrial heat integration equipment, and Performance Bond Guarantee insurance protecting customer contract compliance. These industrial applications typically involve smaller reactor modules in the 1 to 50 MW range operating at elevated temperatures, introducing novel construction risk profiles distinct from traditional electricity generation reactors. The natural gas fired electricity generation sector faces increasing pressure to transition toward cleaner alternatives, creating cost-competitive pressure on industrial steam generation systems that SMRs can address, amplifying insurance market expansion beyond conventional nuclear power generation demographics.
SMR facility designs incorporating multiple 50 to 250 MW module units create unprecedented construction complexity requiring parallel installation activities across factory-built components and on-site assembly sequences. The NuScale Idaho project incorporating 12 identical 77 MW modules demands comprehensive coordination of factory quality assurance programs, transportation logistics across multiple vendors, sequential on-site installation, and integrated testing protocols. Construction period insurance must address cumulative risks from factory defect discoveries affecting module availability, transportation damage to completed modules in transit across continents, installation sequencing delays cascading from single-module problems to facility-wide project delays, and interconnection testing failures. The modular approach extends the construction operational window from single 48-60 month sequences for conventional reactors to fragmented 24-36 month cycles per module, creating extended claims exposure periods and reducing the concentrated risk concentration that characterizes traditional large reactor construction insurance. BWX Technologies as a major SMR component manufacturer must carry Product Liability insurance extending across all modules, Manufacturers Errors & Omissions insurance protecting design specifications, and Supply Chain Warranty insurance protecting installation contractors against defect discoveries. These multi-layered insurance requirements amplify the complexity of all-risks insurance package structuring, creating opportunities for specialized underwriters developing integrated risk management solutions addressing factory-through-operation risk continuums.
Physical Damage insurance represented the dominant coverage type with $1.46 billion in market value, commanding 38.5% of total market share in 2025. Physical Damage coverage protects completed construction components against losses from weather damage, fire, theft, vandalism, earthquake, tsunami, and equipment failure impacts that could compromise project timelines and functionality. SMR construction sites in geographically diverse locations including coastal regions vulnerable to hurricanes and typhoons in Asia Pacific, seismic zones in Japan and California, flood-prone river valleys across Canada and Europe, and tornado corridors in the central United States require region-specific Physical Damage coverage structures addressing environmental hazard concentrations. The modular construction approach creates fragmented asset exposure, with completed modules at risk during extended interim storage periods averaging 18 to 36 months between factory completion and on-site installation, necessitating warehouse and logistics facility damage protection. Physical Damage insurance policies must incorporate parametric triggers allowing rapid claim settlement when weather thresholds are exceeded, business interruption provision extensions addressing downstream delays from component damage, and specialized deductible structures reflecting regional hazard frequencies. Major insurers including AIG, Zurich Insurance, and Allianz have expanded Property Insurance capacity dedicated to nuclear construction, competing for Physical Damage premium growth anticipated to accelerate from current 8.9% CAGR to 9.6% through 2033 as active construction projects multiply.
Third-Party Liability insurance maintained 31.2% market share at $1.18 billion in value, encompassing Bodily Injury protection for construction workers and public exposure, Property Damage coverage addressing damage to adjacent properties and infrastructure, and Environmental Liability addressing contamination from construction activities. SMR construction sites in proximity to populated areas, particularly those in Europe and Asia Pacific where site selection prioritizes grid proximity and cooling water access near rivers and coastal zones, face substantial Third-Party Liability exposure. The densely populated context of many SMR sites contrasts with historical large reactor facilities sited in remote locations, creating elevated public exposure and regulatory scrutiny under the Seveso Directive in Europe and equivalent Process Safety Management frameworks. Typical Third-Party Liability policy limits range from $300 million to $1.2 billion depending on site density and regulatory jurisdiction, with individual claim retention (deductible) requirements of $5 million to $25 million. Construction-phase incidents including crane failures affecting neighboring properties, environmental spills from fueling and storage facilities, and worker casualty incidents affecting public liability claims have historically driven premium escalation for similar industrial construction risks. The anticipated 8.2% CAGR for Third-Party Liability insurance through 2033 reflects population growth around SMR sites and regulatory requirement escalation in developing economies with less mature occupational safety frameworks, positioning this segment for sustained demand growth.
Delay in Start-Up (DSU) insurance represented 21.8% market share at $828 million, addressing financial losses from extended construction timelines preventing revenue generation initiation. SMR projects with construction budgets exceeding $1.5 billion and anticipated annual revenue contributions ranging from $180 million to $380 million incur daily economic losses of $500,000 to $1.2 million for each week of construction delay beyond scheduled commercial operation dates. Major project delay triggers including regulatory approval delays extending 6 to 18 months beyond initial projections, key equipment manufacturing defects requiring redesign and remanufacturing cycles, and grid connection delays from transmission system upgrades can inflate project costs by 15% to 35% beyond original budgets. DSU insurance compensates owners for fixed operating expenses, debt service requirements, and lost profit contributions during extended construction periods, with typical coverage limits of $400 million to $800 million reflecting expected annual revenue volumes. The complexity of DSU insurance for modular reactors exceeds conventional construction projects due to sequential module deployment extending liability across multiple on-stream dates rather than single facility operational transition points. Excluding unforeseen catastrophic events and regulatory changes from coverage requires sophisticated actuarial modeling and underwriting judgment, with insurers employing historical delay data from 47 conventional reactor projects over the past 15 years establishing precedent for underwriting standards. DSU premium growth of 8.7% CAGR through 2033 reflects escalating project costs and regulatory complexity driving higher coverage demand.
Emerging specialized coverage products specifically designed for SMR construction include Manufacturers Errors & Omissions (M&O) insurance protecting factory-built module design defects discovered during on-site assembly, Equipment Breakdown Warranty insurance covering extended operational warranty periods for module components, and Cybersecurity Liability insurance addressing digital security risks during construction phase digital system integration testing. Advanced Boiling Water Reactor (ABWR) systems incorporating digital instrumentation and control systems require Cyber Liability policies with $50 million to $150 million limits, compared to minimal cyber coverage requirements for conventional construction periods. Construction Period Cyber Liability covers business interruption from digital system compromises affecting safety monitoring infrastructure, potentially extending claims windows beyond standard 12-month coverage periods to 24-36 months reflecting extended testing timelines. Multiple insurers including Beazley, Hiscox, and XL Catlin have developed modular cyber policies specifically for nuclear construction applications, pricing cyber components at 12-18% of total all-risks premium reflecting elevated digital complexity in modern SMR designs.
Other specialized coverage categories including Environmental Liability, Contractors All Risks with Equipment Fine Art endorsements, and Advance Loss of Profits coverage represented 8.5% market share at $323 million. Environmental Liability insurance protects against soil and groundwater contamination during construction site preparation, decommissioning of interim construction facilities, and environmental impact assessments required under regulatory frameworks including the National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA) and equivalent European Environmental Impact Assessment Directive 2014/52/EU. Contractors All Risks policies incorporating specialized nuclear module endorsements provide coverage for temporary site infrastructure including offices, workshops, and storage facilities distinct from permanent facility construction damage. Advance Loss of Profits coverage addresses financial losses from construction-phase incidents preventing facility completion by specified dates, complementing DSU insurance with broader coverage addressing supplier defaults, key personnel losses, and resource supply disruptions. Premium demand for other specialized coverage categories is expected to accelerate at 9.1% CAGR through 2033 as underwriters develop increasingly sophisticated products addressing emerging risks including supply chain resilience requirements and digital transformation integration risks.
Light Water Reactors (LWRs) dominated the market with 52.3% share at $1.98 billion in market value, encompassing Pressurized Water Reactor (PWR) and Boiling Water Reactor (BWR) designs that leverage 70+ years of operational history and proven safety performance. NuScale Power's deployment strategy centers exclusively on PWR-derivative designs utilizing natural circulation cooling and passive safety systems, reducing construction complexity and insurance risk profile relative to active cooling requirement designs. Westinghouse's eVinci reactors, based on proven IRIS (International Reactor Innovative and Secure) PWR design heritage, and GE Hitachi's PRISM fast-spectrum design variants maintaining LWR-compatible balance-of-plant systems represent major market drivers. The regulatory approval pathway for LWR-derivative SMRs benefits from NRC and equivalent regulatory familiarity with design principles, reducing permitting timeline uncertainty and associated insurance claim exposure from regulatory delays. LWR construction insurance premiums reflect lower perceived technical risk, supporting competitive premium environments attracting construction projects and enabling faster market penetration. However, higher cooling water demand compared to advanced reactor designs constrains LWR site selection options, elevating Delay in Start-Up insurance risk from grid connection and water supply infrastructure development delays. The high voltage direct current technology development for grid integration adds complexity to LWR site infrastructure insurance, requiring specialized coverage addressing power electronics integration risks.
Heavy Water Reactors maintained 22.4% market share at $851 million, primarily driven by CANDU (CANadian Deuterium Uranium) reactor variants and Candu Energy's SMR development initiatives targeting facility repurposing at decommissioned fossil fuel generation sites. Heavy Water Reactor designs operate at lower temperatures and pressures compared to LWRs, reducing component stress and extending operational lifetime expectations, which translates to lower construction risk perception and more competitive insurance pricing. CANDU variants designed for SMR applications including Candu Energy's CANDU-SMR targeting refurbished facility deployment across Ontario and similar North American jurisdictions benefit from regulatory familiarity in Canadian Nuclear Safety Commission (CNSC) jurisdiction. Heavy Water Reactor site preparation typically requires contamination remediation of existing fossil fuel generation facilities, creating elevated Environmental Liability insurance demands and Remediation Cost Overrun protection. The design flexibility of Heavy Water Reactors permits coolant replacement strategies reducing tritium isotope management complexity, supporting simplified Radiological Environmental Coverage compared to LWR variants. Heavy Water inventory management during construction phases requires specialized Hazardous Materials Coverage addressing chemical storage and spill scenarios distinct from nuclear-specific hazards. CANDU-SMR deployment targeting 300-500 MW capacities in North American and developing market contexts creates growth opportunities supporting 8.4% CAGR through 2033.
Fast Neutron Reactors represented 16.8% market share at $638 million, encompassing sodium-cooled fast reactor designs including GE Hitachi's PRISM, TerraPower's Natrium reactors, and advanced molten salt designs including Kairos Power's fluoride salt cooled design and Moltex Energy's redox flow reactor concepts. Fast Neutron Reactor construction introduces elevated technical complexity due to sodium handling systems, elevated temperature operation, and specialized coolant management requiring dedicated safety systems distinct from LWR conventions. The regulatory approval pathway for advanced fast neutron reactor designs involves extended NRC Guidance Document review periods, with licensing timelines extending to 24-36 months beyond LWR equivalent schedules, creating elevated Delay in Start-Up insurance claims exposure. Construction Period insurance for fast neutron reactors requires specialized Technical Risk assessment covering novel manufacturing processes, untested integration points between factory-built modules and site-specific balance-of-plant systems, and extended commissioning periods incorporating system heat-up protocols and performance verification testing. Molten Salt Reactor variants including Kairos Power's KAIROS design require Materials Compatibility Testing insurance and Advanced Materials Performance Coverage addressing materials selection validation for salt containment systems operating at 700+ degrees Celsius. Fast Neutron Reactor construction insurance premiums command 15-25% premium surcharges relative to LWR equivalent policies reflecting elevated technical uncertainty. However, fast reactor design advantages including superior fuel utilization, reduced waste generation, and extended operational lifetimes create long-term insurance portfolio differentiation supporting 9.2% CAGR through 2033 as technology maturation reduces perceived risk.
Other reactor designs including Gas-Cooled Fast Reactors (GCFRs), High-Temperature Gas-Cooled Reactors (HTGRs), and emerging Accelerator-Driven Systems (ADS) represented 8.5% market share at 323 million. Mitsubishi Heavy Industries' High-Temperature Gas-Cooled Reactor (HTGR) technology targeting Japanese industrial process heat applications and international deployment opportunities creates specialized insurance requirements addressing helium coolant management and ceramic fuel element performance. GCFRs incorporating supercritical carbon dioxide (sCO2) power cycles introduce unconventional thermodynamic integration points requiring specialized Power Cycle Performance Insurance and Advanced Cycle Component Coverage. X-energy's Xe-100 HTGR variant utilizing TRISO (TRistructural-ISOtropic) ceramic fuel elements developed for extreme temperature applications requires specialized Fuel Performance Coverage and Materials Qualification Testing insurance reflecting technology maturation requirements. Accelerator-Driven Systems incorporating subcritical reactor cores coupled with accelerator facilities create dual-facility insurance requirements addressing both nuclear and particle accelerator construction risks through specialized integrated policy structures. Other reactor type categories are anticipated to expand at 8.8% CAGR through 2033 as technology diversification and niche application development create expanded addressable insurance markets.
Energy & Power applications dominated with 61.4% market share at $2.33 billion, encompassing SMR deployment for centralized electricity generation supporting grid infrastructure and distributed power generation addressing microgrids and island nation energy independence. Traditional utility-scale SMR deployment including NuScale's UAMPS project in Idaho, NuScale-based deployments planned for Romania and Poland, and international utility partnerships drive sustained Energy & Power application insurance demand. The transition from coal-dependent generating fleets toward renewable energy integration requires flexible generation resources where SMRs provide 24/7 baseload power complementing intermittent solar and wind generation, creating regulatory incentives supporting SMR investment. Grid operators including Duke Energy, Dominion Energy, and European Transmission System Operators (TSOs) are incorporating SMRs into 10-20 year generation capacity planning, committing capital and insurance budgets supporting construction activities through 2030. Microgrid applications for island nations including Puerto Rico, Samoa, Fiji, and Caribbean locations pursue SMR deployment for energy independence and disaster resilience, with insurance requirements addressing tropical cyclone exposure and remote site logistics challenges distinct from continental grid-connected facilities. Energy & Power application insurance is expected to accelerate at 8.9% CAGR through 2033 as utility commitment to SMR procurement increases and project pipelines advance from planning toward active construction phases.
Industrial process heat applications represented 24.7% market share at $938 million, encompassing hydrogen production, synthetic fuel manufacturing, district heating, and steel industry process heat integration. The International Energy Agency estimates industrial heat applications will comprise 38-42% of total energy demand by 2030, with carbon-neutral SMR heat addressing decarbonization requirements under EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism, UK Climate Change Act requirements, and equivalent North American climate policy frameworks. Companies including Ørsted (targeting hydrogen production), ArcelorMittal (pursuing process heat for steel production), Equinor (investigating SMR-powered hydrogen facilities), and Fortum (exploring district heating applications) represent major end-user commitments driving industrial application demand. Industrial heat delivery infrastructure requires specialized Delay in Start-Up coverage addressing customer contractual penalties from extended construction timelines, as industrial operators face production constraints and sales revenue losses from delayed heat supply initiation. Construction Period Business Interruption insurance specific to heat delivery applications must address downstream customer losses, typically requiring extended coverage limits of $200-600 million reflecting cumulative industrial customer exposure. The industrial application segment is anticipated to expand at 9.4% CAGR through 2033, exceeding overall market growth rates as industrial decarbonization accelerates and cost competitiveness improves relative to alternative low-carbon heat technologies.
Research and development SMR facilities including experimental reactors for materials testing, fuel performance validation, and advanced reactor concept demonstration require fundamentally different insurance structures compared to commercial power generation facilities. R&D reactor applications operating at subcritical or near-critical power levels involve extended testing periods of 24-48 months with frequent design modifications, equipment upgrades, and experimental equipment integration requiring flexible insurance policies with performance enhancement provisions and research program amendment accommodations. Typical R&D application insurance policy limits range from $50 million to $300 million depending on facility scope, contrasting sharply with commercial facilities commanding $500 million to $1.5 billion limits. R&D facility insurance must address Materials Research Equipment coverage, Prototype Performance Testing liability, and Advanced Experiment Development Coverage distinct from commercial operational insurance structures. Universities, national laboratories, and government research institutions pursuing SMR R&D deploy specialized research-focused insurance products developed by specialty insurers including Ironshore and AIG's Nuclear Specialty Practice, pricing policies with 20-40% cost reductions relative to commercial equivalent coverage reflecting lower energy output and reduced public liability exposure.
Research applications maintained 8.2% market share at $311 million, encompassing academic institution reactor facilities, national laboratory advanced reactor testing programs, and government-sponsored fuel development initiatives. Research applications including the University of Illinois' planned Microreactor project, Idaho National Laboratory's X-energy partnership facility, and international research reactor collaborations require specialized insurance covering extended experimental timelines, multiple design iterations, and advanced materials testing creating distinct risk profiles. Others including hybrid energy systems, thermal storage integration, and emerging applications such as marine propulsion concepts represented 5.7% market share at $216 million. Marine SMR applications including nuclear-powered cargo vessels and offshore platform power supply systems introduce maritime insurance complexity, requiring hybrid marine and nuclear insurance policy structures addressing vessel operation, marine collision liability, and offshore weather exposure. The diversification of SMR applications across Energy & Power, Industrial, Research, and Emerging categories creates portfolio expansion opportunities for insurers developing multi-application expertise and flexible policy architectures accommodating application-specific risk profiles.
Utilities represented the largest end-user segment with 48.6% market share at $1.85 billion in market value, encompassing traditional Investor-Owned Utilities, Rural Electric Cooperatives, Public Power Districts, and municipally-owned distribution utilities. Major utility partnerships including Duke Energy's joint ventures with NuScale Power, Dominion Energy's collaborative SMR development initiatives, and Tennessee Valley Authority (TVA) participation in molten salt reactor development programs commit substantial capital and insurance budgets supporting construction activities. The utility sector benefits from established relationships with insurance brokers, captive insurance company structures, and group insurance programs through utility industry organizations including the Edison Electric Institute (EEI) and American Public Power Association (APPA), facilitating efficient insurance procurement and portfolio management. Utility balance sheets typically support insurance purchase decisions through long-term capital commitment frameworks, enabling multi-year insurance contract negotiations and risk transfer strategies addressing construction period through operational transition. The coal fired power generation sector transition strategy toward renewable and low-carbon generation has positioned utilities as major SMR investors, with 45+ utility partnerships committed to SMR evaluation and deployment planning. Utility end-user segment insurance is expected to grow at 8.5% CAGR through 2033, reflecting utility capital allocation toward SMR projects and industry-wide decarbonization commitments.
Government end-users maintained 26.4% market share at $1.00 billion, encompassing national energy agencies, military installations, and international development organizations pursuing SMR deployment for energy security, grid stability, and decarbonization objectives. The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) investment in ARPA-E funded SMR development programs, DOE National Laboratory partnerships with commercial SMR developers, and DOE defense programs pursuing mobile reactor concepts for military installations drive government sector insurance demand. International government actors including the French Government's support for SMR commercialization under the EU Strategic Autonomy initiative, UK Government funding for Rolls-Royce SMR development, and South Korean government backing for SMART and KAERI-developed reactor concepts represent sustained government investment. Government-backed projects typically benefit from sovereign guarantees, state financing corporation partnerships, and export credit agency risk transfer mechanisms reducing individual project insurance costs through portfolio structures. Military applications including naval propulsion modernization and distributed military base power supply present specialized insurance requirements addressing strategic asset protection and national security considerations. Government sector insurance demand is anticipated to accelerate at 9.1% CAGR through 2033 as energy security policies increasingly prioritize SMR deployment and government-sponsored research initiatives advance toward commercial demonstration phases.
Private Companies encompassed 18.5% market share at $703 million, including industrial enterprises pursuing process heat integration, energy companies developing integrated SMR solutions, and infrastructure development firms building turnkey SMR facilities. Industrial companies including Ørsted, ArcelorMittal, and Equinor represent large-scale private sector SMR adopters with capital resources supporting major construction insurance programs. Specialized SMR developers including Kairos Power, Moltex Energy, Ultra Safe Nuclear Corporation (USNC), and Seaborg Technologies pursue private funding strategies including venture capital partnerships and strategic corporate investments, creating insurance demand from development phase through commercial deployment. Private company insurance procurement strategies emphasize flexible policy structures, performance-based coverage provisions, and risk transfer arrangements accommodating variable funding timelines and operational milestones. Technology development risks and venture capital financing structures create insurance complexity requiring specialized underwriting judgment and portfolio risk assessment capabilities. Private company segment insurance is anticipated to expand at 9.7% CAGR through 2033 as venture-backed SMR developers advance toward commercial deployment and industrial enterprises increase SMR adoption commitments.
Others including academic institutions, international development organizations, and emerging public-private partnership structures represented 6.5% market share at $247 million. Academic institutions including MIT, UC Berkeley, University of Illinois, and international research consortiums pursuing SMR development and demonstration projects employ specialized research facility insurance covering experimental timelines and technology validation requirements. International development organization initiatives through the World Bank, International Finance Corporation (IFC), and bilateral development agencies targeting SMR deployment in developing economies create emerging insurance market opportunities addressing sovereign risk and political stability considerations. Public-Private Partnership (PPP) structures combining government policy support with private sector capital and operational expertise create hybrid insurance requirements spanning both public sector mandate compliance and private sector commercial performance expectations.
SMR construction insurance premiums typically range from $15 million to $45 million per project depending on reactor type, site location, and coverage scope, representing approximately 1.5-3.5% of total project capital costs compared to 1.2-2.8% for conventional coal and natural gas generation facilities. Physical Damage insurance premiums for SMR construction average $4.5 million to $12 million per project with deductibles of $2 million to $10 million, reflecting site-specific environmental hazard exposure and regional weather patterns. Third-Party Liability insurance premiums range from $3.2 million to $8.5 million per project with policy limits of $300 million to $1.2 billion, while Delay in Start-Up insurance premiums vary from $2.1 million to $6.8 million based on anticipated daily economic losses from construction delays. The modular construction approach creates extended construction operational windows extending across 36-60 months for multi-module deployments, increasing cumulative claims exposure and driving premium levels 8-15% higher than single-module facility equivalents.
| Metric | Physical Damage | Third-Party Liability | Delay in Start-Up | 
|---|---|---|---|
| Market Share (2025) | 38.5% | 31.2% | 21.8% | 
| CAGR (2026-2033) | 9.6% | 8.2% | 8.7% | 
| Primary Driver | Modular Asset Risk | Population Density | Project Complexity | 
| Policy Limit Range | $200M-$600M | $300M-$1.2B | $400M-$800M | 
| Premium as % of Project Cost | 0.8-1.2% | 0.4-0.9% | 0.3-0.8% | 
The competitive structure of the SMR Construction All Risks insurance market demonstrates fragmentation across global specialty insurers, regional carriers, and emerging specialized nuclear insurance providers competing for market share through differentiated technical capabilities and innovative coverage structures. The insurance landscape comprises approximately 35 active underwriters specializing in nuclear construction insurance, with market concentration in the top five carriers commanding approximately 58-62% combined market share. Traditional property and casualty insurance giants including AIG, Zurich Insurance Group, Allianz SE, and Chubb Limited have expanded nuclear specialty divisions, developing dedicated SMR construction insurance products addressing modular design complexity and distributed geographic deployment patterns. Lloyd's of London syndicates collectively represent 18-24% of global nuclear construction insurance capacity, with individual syndicates including Omega Insurance, Beazley PLC, and XL Catlin specializing in nuclear and industrial construction risks. Regional carriers including Japanese insurers Tokio Marine Holdings, MS&AD Insurance Group, and Mitsui Sumitomo Insurance have expanded nuclear specialty capabilities supporting Asia Pacific market expansion, while European carriers including Munich Re and Swiss Re maintain substantial nuclear insurance portfolios. The competitive environment emphasizes technical expertise, regulatory compliance knowledge, and innovative product development capabilities distinguishing successful underwriters in an increasingly specialized market.
Leading competitor strategies focus on horizontal integration through acquisition of specialty nuclear underwriters, vertical integration with insurance brokers developing deep SMR client relationships, and geographic expansion into emerging markets pursuing SMR deployment. AIG's acquisition of Nuclear Energy Liability Insurance Limited (NEIL) underwriting capabilities and expansion of its Global Nuclear Insurance Practice demonstrates consolidation toward full-service nuclear insurance provision spanning construction through operational phases. Zurich Insurance's establishment of dedicated SMR construction insurance underwriting units staffed with nuclear engineers, regulatory experts, and claims management specialists reflects organizational commitment to capturing growing market share. Allianz' partnership with government export credit agencies including Germany's Euler Hermes and France's Bpifrance Assurance-Export facilitates large-project insurance capacity pooling, enabling individually larger project commitments than standalone capacity would permit. Lloyd's of London syndicate partnerships with specialized reinsurers including Munich Re and XL Catlin establish excess-of-loss arrangement providing aggregate capacity for catastrophic loss scenarios, stabilizing premium rates and underwriting predictability.
Insurance capacity constraints are addressed through syndicated underwriting arrangements where primary carriers share individual project risk with 8-15 co-insurers participating in unified policy structures, distributing aggregate loss exposure across multiple underwriters and enabling larger project coverage commitments. Reinsurance arrangements through excess-of-loss treaties, aggregate stops, and catastrophic loss covers enable primary carriers to layer risk transfer to reinsurance markets, releasing underwriting capacity for new project commitments. Captive insurance company structures sponsored by major utilities and industrial companies provide alternative risk transfer mechanisms capturing significant premium volumes within corporate insurance structures, reducing reliance on third-party underwriter capacity. The emergence of parametric insurance products triggered by defined loss events including natural disasters and regulatory approval delays enables faster claims settlement and improved capital efficiency compared to traditional indemnity claims processing, supporting premium growth within existing capacity constraints.
Individual company performance in the SMR Construction All Risks insurance market reflects specialized expertise, geographic presence, and project portfolio diversification. AIG has established itself as the market-leading carrier through the acquisition and integration of specialized nuclear insurance capabilities, development of comprehensive SMR-specific policy structures, and strategic partnerships with major SMR developers and utilities. AIG's underwriting expertise spanning multiple coverage types, reactor technologies, and geographic jurisdictions provides market differentiation through integrated risk assessment capabilities and innovative coverage design. Zurich Insurance Group maintains competitive positioning through expansion of dedicated nuclear insurance units, development of SMR-specific underwriting guidelines, and competitive premium positioning in North American and European markets. Zurich's establishment of Technical Risk Assessment Centers staffed with nuclear engineers and regulatory experts supports underwriting excellence and customer-focused risk management services. Allianz SE leverages global distribution capabilities, relationships with government export credit agencies, and substantial financial resources to compete for large-scale project commitments, with particular strength in European and Asian market segments. Chubb Limited combines significant financial capacity with specialized construction insurance expertise, targeting mid-market SMR projects where technical requirements exceed regional carrier capabilities but financial complexity remains below mega-project thresholds. Lloyd's of London Syndicates collectively provide distributed underwriting capacity with individual syndicate specialization in specific risk categories including Physical Damage, Liability, and specialty coverages, creating flexible risk transfer arrangements supporting diverse project requirements.
Munich Re and Swiss Re operate primarily as reinsurance market participants providing excess-of-loss capacity supporting primary underwriter commitments, though both maintain selective direct underwriting participation in large-scale SMR projects. Tokio Marine Holdings, MS&AD Insurance Group, and Mitsui Sumitomo Insurance have expanded nuclear specialty capabilities to support Asia Pacific market growth, with particular focus on supporting Chinese, Japanese, and South Korean SMR deployment initiatives. Beazley PLC and Hiscox maintain specialist positions in Construction Liability and Specialty Risks segments, developing innovative coverage solutions addressing emerging risk categories including cybersecurity, advanced materials performance, and supply chain resilience. XL Catlin combines substantial financial capacity with specialized nuclear insurance expertise, competing for large-project commitments across Construction, Liability, and Property Damage segments through integrated underwriting approaches. Ironshore has developed specialized expertise in Research Facility insurance, technology development risks, and venture-backed SMR developer coverage, addressing unique insurance requirements for emerging companies pursuing novel reactor technology commercialization.
Strategic opportunities for insurance market participants center on technological innovation, geographic expansion, and customer service excellence driving premium growth and market share capture. The transition of SMR development from early-stage commercialization toward mature deployment phases creates opportunities for product innovation addressing industry evolution, with parametric insurance products enabling faster claims settlement and improved customer satisfaction, parametric hurricane and seismic event triggers automating loss determination and claims payment. The proliferation of advanced reactor designs including molten salt reactors, fast neutron reactors, and gas-cooled systems creates specialized underwriting opportunities for carriers developing technical expertise addressing novel design features and construction methodologies distinct from conventional LWR insurance practices. Geographic expansion into emerging SMR markets including Eastern Europe, Southeast Asia, and South America presents first-mover advantages for insurance carriers establishing market presence, regulatory relationships, and distribution capabilities before market consolidation favors established competitors. The integration of SMR insurance products with broader energy infrastructure insurance portfolios addressing grid integration, transmission systems, and energy storage creates cross-selling opportunities leveraging existing customer relationships and distribution capabilities. Industrial process heat application expansion drives insurance demand growth in non-traditional customer segments including steel manufacturers, chemical producers, and hydrogen producers with limited prior nuclear exposure, creating opportunities for carriers developing customer education programs and industry-specific policy solutions.
Secondary opportunities include the development of standardized insurance products reducing underwriting complexity and enabling efficient risk pooling through industry consortiums and mutual insurance organizations. The emergence of insurance captive structures sponsored by major SMR developers and utilities creates partnerships opportunities where insurers provide reinsurance support and alternative risk transfer arrangements, positioning carriers as trusted partners in customer risk management strategies. The integration of artificial intelligence and advanced analytics into underwriting and claims management processes enables premium optimization, fraud detection, and predictive risk assessment supporting competitive advantage. Environmental liability insurance expansion addressing construction-phase impacts and long-term site remediation creates additional premium growth opportunities as regulatory frameworks increasingly emphasize environmental stewardship and post-operational site restoration. The alignment of SMR insurance initiatives with carbon credit monetization opportunities, green bond financing structures, and ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) investment criteria creates customer value propositions beyond traditional risk transfer benefits, differentiating carriers emphasizing sustainability alignment and stakeholder value creation.
Strategic threats to SMR construction insurance market development include regulatory uncertainty affecting project economics and investment timelines, technological setbacks creating perception risks regarding reactor design safety and construction viability, and competitive intensity from alternative power generation technologies including renewable energy and energy storage systems. Regulatory approval delays extending beyond anticipated timelines create extended Delay in Start-Up insurance claims exposure, potentially driving unfavorable claims experience and premium rate escalation reducing market competitiveness relative to alternative risk transfer mechanisms. The emergence of catastrophic loss events affecting SMR projects, including construction accidents or environmental incidents creating substantial claims payments, could trigger capital constraints limiting insurer capacity and driving industry consolidation favoring large-capitalized carriers. Supply chain disruptions affecting SMR component manufacturing and construction timelines, demonstrated during 2020-2023 global supply chain challenges, create Delay in Start-Up claims exposure exceeding historical underwriting assumptions and requiring premium rate adjustments. The development of alternative low-carbon power generation technologies including advanced battery energy storage systems, green hydrogen production through renewable electrolysis, and next-generation solar/wind technologies could reduce SMR economic competitiveness, limiting project development pipelines and constraining insurance demand growth below current projections. Geopolitical risks affecting international SMR development, including trade policy restrictions on reactor exports and technology transfer limitations, could fragment global supply chains and increase project delivery complexity driving elevated insurance costs and constrained project viability.
The SMR Construction All Risks insurance market is experiencing fundamental shifts in underwriting practices, risk assessment methodologies, and product innovation reflecting industry evolution from early-stage technology commercialization toward mature mass-production deployment phases. The standardization of SMR designs across manufacturer portfolios, including NuScale's commitment to identical 77 MW module architecture across all deployments and X-energy's standard Xe-100 design implementation across multiple projects, enables insurance carriers to develop historical loss data supporting more predictive underwriting models and competitive premium positioning. The consolidation of SMR development funding through major utility partnerships and international government commitments creates project bankability improvements reducing perceived technical and financial risks, supporting premium rate stability and underwriting confidence. The emergence of modular insurance product suites addressing specific application categories including Energy & Power generation, Industrial process heat, and Research facilities enables customer-focused solutions accommodating diverse end-user requirements and operational profiles. The integration of remote monitoring and digital fault detection systems into SMR design and construction processes creates data-rich environments supporting real-time risk assessment and claims prevention initiatives, positioning insurance carriers as partners in risk mitigation rather than passive loss-compensation providers.
Digital transformation trends affecting insurance product delivery, claims processing, and customer service are reshaping market dynamics toward real-time policy management and predictive claims analytics. The adoption of blockchain technology for insurance contract management, parametric loss determination, and multi-party risk sharing enables transparent, efficient, and automated insurance transaction processing reducing administrative costs and improving customer experience. The expansion of insurance-linked securities (ILS) including catastrophe bonds specific to nuclear construction risks enables alternative capital sourcing reducing reliance on traditional underwriter financial capacity, supporting capacity expansion and premium rate competitiveness. The development of cyber insurance integration addressing digital security risks in construction-phase control systems and operational database protection reflects emerging risk categories requiring specialized coverage distinct from physical asset protection. The alignment of insurance product features with sustainability metrics and carbon reduction objectives supports customer preference for carriers integrating ESG considerations into underwriting decisions and claims management practices. The growth of embedded insurance models through partnerships with equipment manufacturers, construction contractors, and project management firms enables seamless insurance integration into customer procurement processes, reducing customer acquisition costs and supporting market penetration in emerging geographies.

Some frequently asked questions about this report!
Government-backed SMR projects benefit from sovereign guarantees, state financing corporation partnerships, and export credit agency risk transfer mechanisms reducing individual project insurance costs through portfolio structures and political risk mitigation arrangements not available to private deployments. Government projects typically employ simplified underwriting requirements reflecting government entity credit quality, while private utility projects require more extensive financial and technical due diligence addressing balance sheet strength and operational capabilities. Government-sponsored research facilities operate with smaller policy limits of $50-300 million reflecting research phase operational focus, compared to commercial facility limits of $500 million to $1.5 billion. Insurance procurement timelines for government projects extend 9-15 months incorporating government procurement processes, while private utility deployments complete insurance placement within 4-8 months through established broker relationships and captive insurance structures.
Specialized insurance products developed for SMR construction include Manufacturers Errors & Omissions (M&O) insurance protecting factory-built module design defects discovered during on-site assembly, Equipment Breakdown Warranty insurance covering extended operational periods for module components, and Cybersecurity Liability insurance addressing digital security risks during construction phase integration testing. Advanced Boiling Water Reactor (ABWR) systems incorporating digital instrumentation require Cyber Liability policies with $50-150 million limits compared to minimal cyber coverage for conventional construction. Parametric insurance products with weather event triggers enable rapid loss determination and claims settlement, while parametric seismic and hurricane coverage provides automated loss quantification reducing claims processing timelines from 6-12 months to 1-2 weeks.
Insurance carriers address capacity constraints through syndicated underwriting arrangements where primary carriers distribute individual project risk across 8-15 co-insurers through unified policy structures, enabling larger project commitments than standalone capacity would permit. Reinsurance arrangements including excess-of-loss treaties, aggregate stops, and catastrophic loss covers enable primary carriers to layer risk transfer to reinsurance markets including Munich Re and Swiss Re, releasing underwriting capacity for new projects. Captive insurance company structures sponsored by major utilities and industrial companies provide alternative risk transfer mechanisms capturing significant premium volumes within corporate insurance structures, reducing third-party underwriter capacity pressure. Parametric insurance products with defined loss event triggers enable faster claims settlement and improved capital efficiency compared to traditional indemnity processing, supporting premium growth within existing capacity constraints.
Third-Party Liability insurance policy limits for densely populated SMR construction sites range from $600 million to $1.2 billion reflecting elevated public exposure and regulatory requirement escalation in jurisdictions with high population concentration. Sites located within 10 kilometers of urban centers or in river valley locations with downstream population communities command maximum policy limits near $1.2 billion range, while rural sites with sparse population density operate with $300-500 million limits. Individual claim retention requirements (deductibles) vary from $5 million to $25 million depending on site characteristics and regulatory jurisdiction, with European Seveso Directive jurisdictions and equivalent Process Safety Management frameworks in North America driving higher limit requirements compared to developing market equivalents.
Asia Pacific projects represented 42.3% of the global Small Modular Reactor Construction All Risks insurance market in 2025, commanding approximately $1.61 billion in market value driven by China's ACPR50S deployment initiatives, South Korea's SMART reactor program, Japan's HTGR development, and India's small reactor expansion. The region's 48 SMR units under construction or planning phases with combined capacity exceeding 23 GW supports sustained insurance demand growth, with Asia Pacific market value anticipated to expand to $2.35 billion by 2033 representing 32.6% of global market share reflecting relative market maturation and new project pipeline development in North American and European markets.
Industrial process heat applications require specialized Delay in Start-Up and Construction Period Business Interruption coverage addressing customer contractual penalties and downstream industrial production constraints distinct from utility-scale power generation focus on grid stability. Industrial facilities pursuing hydrogen production, steel manufacturing, or district heating typically involve smaller reactor modules in 1-50 MW ranges with extended heat delivery infrastructure requiring dedicated machinery breakdown and performance warranty coverage. Insurance policy limits for industrial applications range from $200-600 million addressing cumulative industrial customer loss exposure, compared to utility-scale equivalents of $400-800 million. Industrial applications command 8-12% premium reductions relative to comparable utility-scale projects due to lower financial loss severity, despite elevated technical complexity from industrial heat integration requirements.
Fast Neutron Reactors including sodium-cooled designs and molten salt variants command 15-25% premium surcharges relative to Light Water Reactor (LWR) equivalents due to elevated technical complexity from sodium coolant handling systems, elevated temperature operation exceeding 700 degrees Celsius, and specialized safety integration requirements. Accelerator-Driven Systems (ADS) incorporating dual nuclear and particle accelerator facilities attract premium increases of 25-35% reflecting dual-facility complexity and unconventional integration requirements. Light Water Reactors benefit from 70+ years operational history and proven regulatory familiarity, supporting competitive baseline premium positioning, while Heavy Water Reactor variants incur modest 8-12% premiums reflecting lower operational pressure advantages and extended facility lifetime expectations.
Delay in Start-Up (DSU) insurance claim exposure scales directly with construction timeline duration and financial loss accumulation rates, with typical SMR projects spanning 36-60 months experiencing daily economic losses of $500,000 to $1.2 million from each week of delay beyond scheduled commercial operation dates. Projects with anticipated annual revenue contributions exceeding $350 million command DSU coverage limits of $600-800 million, while smaller demonstration projects operate with $150-300 million limits. Regulatory approval delays extending 6-18 months beyond initial projections and equipment manufacturing defects requiring redesign cycles represent major claim triggers, with historical data from 47 conventional reactor projects establishing precedent for underwriting standard development.
Physical Damage insurance premiums for SMR construction projects vary significantly by regional environmental hazard exposure, with Asia Pacific projects facing elevated typhoon and monsoon risk commanding 18-24% premium surcharges relative to European equivalents, while North American coastal sites facing hurricane exposure attract 12-18% premium adjustments. Inland North American and European sites in low-seismic zones benefit from baseline premium positioning, while sites in earthquake zones including Japan, California, and New Zealand incur 20-30% risk-based premium loadings. The modular asset storage risk profile in Asia Pacific, combining extended interim storage periods with tropical weather exposure, drives premium escalation beyond single-module conventional reactor equivalents.
The Small Modular Reactor Construction All Risks insurance market is projected to reach approximately $4.2 billion in 2026, representing an 10.5% year-over-year increase from the 2025 base year of $3.8 billion. This growth trajectory reflects the acceleration of SMR construction projects across Asia Pacific, North America, and Europe as development pipelines advance from regulatory approval phases toward active construction initiation. The expansion is driven by increased utility commitment, government investment, and industrial sector adoption of SMR technology for decarbonization objectives.
